verificationNote: Risk assessment methodology constructed from IEC 62443-3-2, NIST SP 800-82, and OT security best practices. Not endorsed by any standards body -- verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
title: OT/ICS Risk Assessment Methodology
description: Risk assessment methodology tailored for Operational Technology and Industrial Control Systems. The critical difference from IT risk assessment is that OT risk must account for SAFETY (human life), ENVIRONMENTAL damage, PROCESS INTEGRITY, and AVAILABILITY as primary impact dimensions -- not just the IT-centric CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
keyPrinciple: In OT environments, a cybersecurity incident can cause physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of life. Risk assessment must therefore weight safety and physical consequences above data confidentiality.
standardsAlignment: IEC 62443-3-2 (Security risk assessment for system design)
NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 (Guide to OT Security)
NACSA Act 854 s21 (Risk assessment obligation for NCII operators)
IEC 61511 (Safety Instrumented Systems -- functional safety context)

impactDimensions.description: OT risk assessment uses five impact dimensions. Safety is weighted highest -- any risk with safety impact cannot be accepted without exhaustive mitigation.

dimensions.name: Safety (Human Life)
dimensions.description: Potential for injury or loss of human life. Any risk scoring Level 4 or 5 on safety impact triggers mandatory safety review and cannot be risk-accepted without HAZOP/SIL review.
examples: SIS compromise enabling unsafe process state
Incorrect process variable display leading to unsafe operator action

dimensions.name: Environmental Damage
dimensions.description: Potential for release of hazardous materials, contamination, or ecological damage. Regulated under environmental legislation in addition to cybersecurity law.
examples: Chemical release due to manipulated process controls
Oil/gas pipeline rupture or uncontrolled flaring

dimensions.name: Process Integrity
dimensions.description: Ability to maintain correct and predictable process operation. Loss of process integrity can cascade to safety and environmental impacts.
examples: Historian data manipulation affecting operational decisions
DCS configuration drift causing incorrect control actions

dimensions.name: Operational Availability
dimensions.description: Ability to maintain continuous operation of critical infrastructure. In OT, availability often outweighs confidentiality -- a plant shutdown can cost millions per hour and affect public services.
examples: DDoS on OT network causing loss of control visibility

dimensions.name: Data Confidentiality
dimensions.description: Protection of sensitive OT data from unauthorised disclosure. While less critical than safety in OT, stolen PLC programs and engineering drawings enable sophisticated follow-on attacks.
examples: Theft of PLC program logic enabling Stuxnet-style attack
Exfiltration of network diagrams enabling lateral movement

impactScale.description: 5-level impact scale weighted toward safety and physical consequences. Level 5 represents the worst-case OT scenario -- loss of life or major environmental disaster.

levels.label: Negligible
levels.availability: Momentary disruption (<1 hour), no operational impact
levels.confidentiality: Internal OT data exposed with no operational value to attacker

levels.label: Minor
levels.processIntegrity: Process deviation requiring manual intervention, no equipment damage
levels.availability: Short disruption (1-8 hours), production loss but no cascading impact
levels.confidentiality: Restricted OT data exposed (network diagrams, non-safety configurations)

levels.label: Moderate
levels.processIntegrity: Significant process upset, equipment damage possible, extended recovery
levels.availability: Extended disruption (8-72 hours), significant production or service impact
levels.confidentiality: Confidential OT data exposed (PLC programs, safety system configurations)

levels.label: Major
levels.environmental: Major release requiring external response, offsite impact
levels.processIntegrity: Major equipment damage, extended outage, potential cascading failure
levels.availability: Prolonged disruption (>72 hours), critical infrastructure service failure affecting public
levels.confidentiality: Credential compromise enabling further OT attacks; sensitive data enabling nation-state targeting

levels.label: Catastrophic
levels.safety: Loss of life or major environmental disaster
levels.environmental: Catastrophic release, widespread ecological damage, long-term remediation
levels.processIntegrity: Destruction of critical infrastructure, total loss of process control
levels.availability: Indefinite outage, national-level critical infrastructure impact
levels.confidentiality: Complete OT intellectual property theft enabling replication of attack across sector

likelihoodScale.description: 5-level likelihood scale incorporating OT-specific factors: Purdue Model exposure, connectivity, threat actor capability, and historical precedent.

levels.label: Rare
levels.otContext: Air-gapped system at Purdue Level 0-1, no known threat actor capability, no historical precedent in sector

levels.label: Unlikely
levels.otContext: Isolated OT network with controlled access, limited threat actor interest, few sector precedents

levels.label: Possible
levels.otContext: OT network with IDMZ but IT connectivity, known threat actor interest in sector, ICS-CERT advisories for components in use

levels.label: Likely
levels.otContext: OT network with direct IT connectivity, active threat actor campaigns in sector, unpatched known vulnerabilities

levels.label: Almost Certain
levels.otContext: Internet-exposed OT systems, active exploitation in the wild, commodity malware targeting OT, legacy systems with public exploits

otSpecificRiskFactors.description: Additional factors that must be considered when assessing OT risk -- these do not exist in IT risk assessments.

factors.name: Purdue Model Level
factors.description: Risk increases as attack moves deeper into the Purdue Model (Level 3 toward Level 0). Attacks at Level 0-1 (field devices, SIS) have highest safety impact potential.
factors.guidance: Assets at Purdue Level 0-1 should have impact weighted +1 level for safety dimension.

factors.name: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) Involvement
factors.description: Any risk that could compromise SIS function must be treated as safety-critical. SIS is the last line of defence against catastrophic physical events.
factors.guidance: If SIS is in scope, minimum safety impact is Level 4 (Major). SIS risks cannot be accepted -- they must be mitigated or avoided.

factors.name: Air-Gap and Network Isolation Status
factors.description: True air-gapped systems have lower likelihood but are not immune (Stuxnet). Systems with any connectivity must be assessed for lateral movement paths.
factors.guidance: Verify air-gap claims with physical cable trace. Air-gapped systems reduce likelihood by 1-2 levels. Verify no maintenance laptops bridge the gap.

factors.name: Legacy Protocol Vulnerability
factors.description: OT protocols (Modbus, DNP3, OPC Classic, BACnet, PROFINET) generally lack authentication and encryption. Exploitation requires network access only -- no credentials needed.
factors.guidance: Systems using unauthenticated protocols should have likelihood weighted +1 if any network access path exists from IT or remote access.

factors.name: Patch Availability and Applicability
factors.description: Many OT systems cannot be patched due to vendor certification requirements, uptime constraints, or end-of-life status. Unpatchable vulnerabilities require compensating controls.
factors.guidance: For unpatchable assets, likelihood is based on compensating control effectiveness, not on vulnerability age.

factors.name: Cross-System Cascade Potential
factors.description: OT processes are interconnected -- a failure in one system can cascade to others. Risk assessment must consider the blast radius beyond the directly affected system.
factors.guidance: Identify upstream and downstream process dependencies. If a compromised system can affect other zones or sites, assess the cascaded impact.

assessmentProcess.description: Step-by-step OT risk assessment process aligned with IEC 62443-3-2.

steps.name: Define System Under Consideration (SUC)
steps.description: Identify the OT system boundary, all connected components, and all external interfaces. Map to Purdue Model levels.

steps.name: Zone and Conduit Partitioning
steps.description: Partition the SUC into zones (groups of assets with common security requirements) and conduits (communication paths between zones). Assign initial SL-T per zone.

steps.name: Threat Analysis
steps.description: Identify threats per zone using MITRE ATT&CK for ICS, historical incidents, and sector-specific threat intelligence. Consider nation-state, criminal, insider, and accidental threats.

steps.name: Consequence Assessment
steps.description: For each threat-zone combination, assess consequences across all five impact dimensions (safety, environmental, process integrity, availability, confidentiality). Use worst-case credible scenario.

steps.name: Vulnerability Assessment
steps.description: Identify vulnerabilities per zone: unpatched CVEs, legacy protocols, default credentials, missing controls, architectural weaknesses. Use passive scanning only in OT.

steps.name: Risk Calculation
steps.description: Calculate inherent risk (likelihood x impact) using the OT-weighted scales. Apply existing controls to determine residual risk. Flag any safety-critical residual risks.
outputs: Risk register with inherent and residual scores

steps.name: Risk Treatment
steps.description: Assign treatment strategy (mitigate, transfer, avoid, accept) per risk. Safety risks cannot be accepted. Document treatment plans with owner, timeline, and verification criteria.

steps.name: SL-T Validation
steps.description: Verify that the assigned SL-T per zone is sufficient to address the identified risks. Adjust SL-T upward if residual risk exceeds tolerance.

triggers: Significant change to OT architecture or connectivity
New threat intelligence indicating changed threat landscape
